coredata.dc:title: N-doped graphene quantum dot-decorated MOF-derived yolk-shell ZnO/NiO hybrids to boost lithium and sodium ion battery performance
coredata.dc:creator: Bajorowicz, Beata
Wilamowska-Zawłocka, Monika
Lisowski, Wojciech
Żak, Andrzej
Klimczuk, Tomasz
coredata.dc:description: Surface engineering at the nanoscale to obtain robust interface between metal oxides and quantum dots is essential for improving the performance and stability of battery materials. Herein, we designed and prepared novel N-doped graphene quantum dot-modified ZnO/NiO anode materials with a well-defined yolk-shell structure for lithium and sodium-ion batteries. NG QDs were assembled on the ZnO/NiO microspheres using three different coupling strategies: solvothermal, direct adsorption and annealing under N2 atmosphere. The presence of NG QDs deposited on the ZnO/NiO hybrids promoted enhanced electrical conductivity, lower charge-transfer resistance and provides more active sites. As a result, NG-ZnO/NiO_s anode obtained by solvothermal route exhibited high reversible delithiation capacity of 912 mAh/g at 18.6 mA g−1 and excellent cycling performance with the average delithiation capacity of 525 mAh/g at 372 mA g−1 over 400 cycles. Moreover, application of the NG-ZnO/NiO_s elecrode in Na-ion batteries revealed decent electrochemical behavior with capacity values reaching 235 mAh/g at 18.6 mA g−1. Importantly, surface properties, morphology and electrochemical behavior of obtained NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids were dependent on the combination route of NG QDs with ZnO/NiO microspheres indicating that quality of heterojunction between composite components has significant impact on the electrode performance.

head.ce:title: N-doped graphene quantum dot-decorated MOF-derived yolk-shell ZnO/NiO hybrids to boost lithium and sodium ion battery performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface engineering at the nanoscale to obtain robust interface between metal oxides and quantum dots is essential for improving the performance and stability of battery materials. Herein, we designed and prepared novel N-doped graphene quantum dot-modified ZnO/NiO anode materials with a well-defined yolk-shell structure for lithium and sodium-ion batteries. NG QDs were assembled on the ZnO/NiO microspheres using three different coupling strategies: solvothermal, direct adsorption and annealing under N2 atmosphere. The presence of NG QDs deposited on the ZnO/NiO hybrids promoted enhanced electrical conductivity, lower charge-transfer resistance and provides more active sites. As a result, NG-ZnO/NiO_s anode obtained by solvothermal route exhibited high reversible delithiation capacity of 912 mAh/g at 18.6 mA g−1 and excellent cycling performance with the average delithiation capacity of 525 mAh/g at 372 mA g−1 over 400 cycles. Moreover, application of the NG-ZnO/NiO_s elecrode in Na-ion batteries revealed decent electrochemical behavior with capacity values reaching 235 mAh/g at 18.6 mA g−1. Importantly, surface properties, morphology and electrochemical behavior of obtained NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids were dependent on the combination route of NG QDs with ZnO/NiO microspheres indicating that quality of heterojunction between composite components has significant impact on the electrode performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the demand for energy storage systems to enable a wide range applications from portable electronic devices, electric and hybrid vehicles to stationary large-scale energy storage grids is increasing worldwide. Among various energy storage systems, lithium-ion batteries have attracted considerable attention since their successful commercialization by Sony in 1991, because of their high energy density and high working voltage [1,2]. On the other hand, recently sodium-ion batteries have been regarded as the next generation battery system and promising alternative to Li-ion batteries due to their similar electrochemical characteristic, low cost, easy availability of sodium and higher feasibility of large-scale energy storage applications [3–7]. Importantly, although many attempts have been made to improve the performance of battery systems, development of novel efficient electrode materials for Li/Na-ion batteries is still a great challenge.

Recently, porous nanostructures-derived from metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are regarded as advanced electrode materials with well-designed structures to achieve enhanced electrochemical performance in battery applications [8–10]. Sun et al. [11] demonstrated that ZIF-67 MOF can be used as both the precursor and sacrificial template to obtain NiCo2O4/NiO hollow dodecahedrons. The as-prepared nanostructures showed excellent performance as lithium-ion battery anode materials which was ascribed to the hierarchical nanostructures that endow high specific surface area, accommodation of volume fluctuation upon Li-ion insertion and increased electron transport properties [11]. In other study, Zhangetal. [12] reported synthesis of GeO2 nanostructures derived from Ge-MOFs for Li-ion battery using calcination in air. The as-obtained material revealed improved reversible capacity and excellent cyclability due to stable nanosheets structure derived from MOFs which favor lithium-ion diffusion and penetration of electrolyte [12]. While various MOF-derived hybrids have been explored as electrode materials for Li-ion batteries, study on their electrochemical performance for Na-ion batteries has received much less attention. Kaneti et al. [13] prepared Ni-doped Co/CoO/NC hybrids for sodium-ion battery by direct calcination of bimetallic Ni-Co-ZIF under argon atmosphere. The promising electrochemical performance of as-fabricated materials was explained by synergistic effects of various factors such as: the presence of many micro- and mesopores that promote sodium-ion insertion/de-insertion, higher amount of electroactive site and the presence of carbon matrix which prevents aggregation of nanoparticles during cycling [13].

Moreover, modification of electrode materials with quantum dots can be an effective approach to further improve the electrochemical performance of Na/Li-ion batteries [14–17]. Various types of quantum dots have been prepared and applied in energy storage devices, including ZnO [18], MnO [19], SnO2 [20], graphene [21] and carbon [22] nanocrystals. Yu et al. [23] presented a simple method to obtain graphene quantum dot-modified porous carbon derived from MOFs for Li-ion batteries using self-assembly of ZIF-8 with QDs followed by calcination and etching routes. The synergistic effect of GQDs and porous carbon caused good cycle stability and high reversible capacity of anode material [23]. In other study, Fe3O4 quantum dot-modified MoS2 nanosheets on graphite paper were prepared by modified hydrothermal growth method and proposed as promising anode for sodium-ion battery [24]. Importantly, combination method of QDs with other materials may affect properties of resulting hybrids, thus development of an appropriate preparation strategy of QD-decorated electrode materials is highly desired [14,25]. However, influence of coupling method of the composite components on the performance of anode hybrid materials was not well reported and investigated.

In this regards, herein, we designed and prepared novel electrode materials based on ZnO/NiO structures derived from MOFs and N-doped graphene quantum dots by three different coupling strategies for the first time. The ZnO/NiO microspheres were obtained from bimetallic Zn, Ni-MOF precursor by simple thermal treatment and then N-doped graphene quantum dots were introduced into yolk-shell ZnO/NiO structures using three various routes: solvothermal, direct adsorption or calcination under N2 atmosphere. The influence of coupling method of yolk-shell NG-ZnO/NiO microspheres on the morphology, structure, surface and electrochemical properties were systematically investigated. The as-prepared hybrids could be employed as anode materials for lithium-ion and sodium-ion batteries and exhibited excellent electrochemical performance.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (99.0 %), nickel(II) nitrate hexahydrate (98.5 %), terephthalic acid (H2BDC, 98 %) and citric acid monohydrate (99.0 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol (EtOH, 99.8 %), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethylene glycol (EG), and urea (pure p.a.) were purchased from POCH S.A. All chemicals were used as received, without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnO/NiO derived Zn-Ni MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: Yolk-shell ZnO/NiO microspheres were prepared by method similar to that described by Liet al. [26] with minor modifications. Briefly, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (1.37 mmol) and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (0.84 mmol) were added into N,N-dimethylformamide (40 mL) and ethylene glycol (25 mL) under continuous stirring for 30 min. After that, terephthalic acid (0.9 mmol) was added to the above solution and stirring was continued for an additional 1 h. The resulting solution mixture was then transferred into the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave which was maintained at 150 °C for 6 h, then cooled to room temperature. The precipitate was washed with DMF and ethanol several times, and dried in the oven at 70 °C for 12 h. The as-synthesized Zn-Ni MOFs were calcined in the oven at 450 °C for 30 min with a heating rate of 2 °C·min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NG QDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure for the synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots [27], citric acid (8 mmol) and urea (24 mmol) were dissolved in deionized water (40 mL). This mixture was stirred for 20 min before it was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The resulting product was collected by adding ethanol and centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained precipitates were dried in vacuum at 80 °C for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NG quantum dot-decorated ZnO/NiO yolk shell hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnO/NiO microspheres and NG QDs powders were combined to form yolk-shell NG QDs-ZnO/NiO hybrids by three different ways: direct adsorption, solvothermal method or calcination route under N2 atmosphere. All prepared samples are presented in Table 1 . Scheme1 depicts preparation processes of NG QDs modified ZnO/NiO composites.

A typical solvothermal process for the preparation of composite was as follows: ZnO/NiO powder (0.3 g) was added into the NG QDs (0.06 g) in solution (ethanol/water 1:1) under vigorous stirring for 1 h, then mixture was placed in a Teflon lined stainless autoclave at 150 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting powder was centrifuged and dried in the oven at 70 °C for 12 h.

The calcination route for the preparation of composite was as follows: ZnO/NiO powder (0.3 g) was dispersed in ethanol/water (1:1) mixture containing NG QDs (0.06 g) under vigorous stirring and maintained for 1 h. Then composites were dried in the oven at 70 °C for 12 h h and treated in N2 at 400 °C for 2 h.

The direct adsorption procedure for the preparation of composite was as follows: ZnO/NiO powder (0.3 g) was added into the NG QDs (0.06 g) dispersed in solution (ethanol/water 1:1) under stirring for 4 h, then, dried in the oven at 70 °C for 12 h.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD, Bruker D2 Phaser diffractometer with a LynxEye-XE detector, Cu Kα radiation λ = 1.54 Å) was used to determine the phase composition of the samples. The morphologies of the obtained samples were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (JEOL JSM-7610F) under high vacuum with an accelerating voltage of 15 kV, and transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi H-800 device, Hitachi High-Technologies) operating at 150 kV. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured using a surface area and pore size analyzer system 3P Micro 200 (3P Instruments GmbH & Co. KG). Specific surface areas were calculated according to the typical Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded using monochromatic Al-Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) from an X-ray source operating at 100 µm spot size, 25 W and 15 kV. The high-resolution (HR) XPS spectra were collected with the analyzer pass energy of 23.5 eV and the energy step size 0.1 eV. Casa XPS software (v.2.3.19, Casa Software Ltd, Wilmslow, United Kigdom) was used to evaluate the XPS data. Binding energy scale was referenced to the C 1 s peak with BE = 284.8 eV. For quantification the PHI Multipak sensitivity factors and determined transmission function of the spectrometer were used. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on a Bruker IFS66V FTIR spectrometer using the potassium bromide pellet technique. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out in argon atmosphere by a Netzsch TG 209 thermogravimetric analyzer from 28 to 1000 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

The electrodes were obtained from the slurry containing 70 wt% of active material (yolk-shell ZnO/NiO or quantum dot-decorated ZnO/NiO hybrids), 10 wt% of binder (polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) Solef 5130, Solvay, Germany) and 20 wt% of conductive additive (Carbon Black C-65 Super P, Imerys Graphite & Carbon, Switzerland), and N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) (Alfa Aesar,) to adjust viscosity. The slurry was casted on a copper foil (10 µm, Copper SE-Cu58 Schlenk Metallfolien GmbH& Co. KG, Germany) using a doctor blade technique. The electrode layers were dried at 80 ⁰C for 24 h, cut into circular electrodes (10 mm in diameter), weighted (mass loading approx. 1 mg cm−1), dried under vacuum at 80 ⁰C for 24 h, and transferred without contact with air into an inert atmosphere glove box (Mbraun Glove Box Systems, Ar atmosphere, H2O < 0.5 ppm, O2 < 0.5 ppm).

For electrochemical tests, Swagelok type half-cells were assembled with the prepared electrodes as working electrodes and metallic lithium or sodium as counter/reference electrodes. Metallic lithium and sodium (99.9 % trace metal basis) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Germany. The electrolytes were 1 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate:dimethyl carbonate, EC:DMC 50:50 wt% + 3 % fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) (E-lyte, Germany) or 1 M NaPF6 in ethylene carbonate:diethyl carbonate, EC:DEC 30:70 wt% + 5 % fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) (E-lyte, Germany). A quartz filter paper (GF-2, 45-µmt hick, Macherey-Nagel GmbH & Co. KG, Germany) was used as a separator.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted on a multichannel battery tester (Neware, China) and a potentiostat–galvanostat (Autolab 302 N, AUTOLAB, Eco Chemie, B.V., Netherlands). The galvanostatic charge–discharge (GDC) cycles were performed using the constant current–constant voltage protocol (CCCV) in the potential range of 0.010 V–3.0 V vs. Li/Li+ (0.010 V–2.7 V vs. Na/Na+) with a 15 min potential hold after the lithiation (sodiation) step. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were conducted with a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 within the same potential range. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were measured in the frequency range from 100 kHz to 10 mHz with the amplitude equal to 5 mV. EIS spectra were recorded at a rest potential equal to 3.2 V vs. Li/Li+ (2.7 V vs. Na/Na+) for the not lithiated (sodiated) electrodes and at approx. 0.2 V for the lithiated (sodiated) electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the NG-ZnO/NiO electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: Series of ZnO/NiO-based electrode materials have been prepared by solvothermal and calcination route followed by coupling with NG quantum dots obtained according to mentioned above various methods. Sample labels, elemental analysis, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore volume of as-prepared samples are presented in Table1.

The content of nitrogen, carbon and hydrogen atoms in the composition of the obtained materials was confirmed by carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen and sulfur elemental analysis (CHNS). N-doped graphene quantum dots consist of 34.24 wt% C, 15.97 wt% N, 6.91 wt% H, the remaining mass fraction can be attributed to oxygen atoms [28]. The presence of nitrogen atoms and higher content of carbon in the case of NG-ZnO/NiO composites as compared with pristine ZnO/NiO indicate successful combination of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with ZnO/NiO material. Interestingly, the highest content of nitrogen and carbon atoms among all obtained hybrids was observed for NG-ZnO/NiO_s sample obtained by solvothermal method (Table 1).

The N2 adsorption–desorption measurements were performed to investigate the BET surface area and pore volume of the prepared samples. The results are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 1 a. The specific surface area of as-prepared materials fluctuated from 1.3 to 16.8 m2·g−1. The ZnO/NiO yolk-shell structure exhibited the largest surface area among all obtained materials and its isotherm was of type IV that indicated the presence of a mesoporous structure. Importantly, mesoporous structure derived from MOFs can facilitate the diffusion of electrolyte and provide free spaces to accommodate the volume fluctuation upon lithium and sodium ions insertion and extraction [8,9]. The BET surface area of N-doped graphene QDs was low (1.3 m2·g−1) but a bit higher than that reported for graphene quantum dots (0.0667 m2·g−1) [29].

The specific surface areas of all NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids were very similar (5.0–5.6 m2·g−1) indicating that combination method of QDs with ZnO/NiO has no significant effect on surface properties. Shrunken surface area of NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids as compared with ZnO/NiO sample could result from the coverage of the exposed intrinsic pores of the MOF-derived structure by the introduced N-doped graphene quantum dots. Moreover, it can be observed that incorporation of quantum dots into ZnO/NiO structure caused decrease in pore volume as compared with unmodified ZnO/NiO microspheres. Similar observation was reported for graphene quantum dot-modified COF and CdS quantum dot-decorated Fe2O3 derived from MOFs [30,31].

Fig. 1b presents the powder X-ray diffraction patterns (PXRD) of ZnO/NiO, NG QDs and quantum dot-modified yolk-shell ZnO/NiO hybrids obtained via three different methods: direct adsorption, solvothermal and calcination routes. The diffraction pattern of unmodified ZnO/NiO revealed the diffraction peaks located at about 31.7°, 34.4°, 36.2°, 47.4°, 56.7° and 62.7° than can be indexed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110) and (103) crystal planes of ZnO, and the distinctive peaks observed at about 36.9°, 42.8° and 62.2° assigned to the (111), (200) and (220) planes of crystalline NiO. The XRD pattern of N-doped graphene quantum dots revealed broad peak at around 26.3°characteristic for nanocrystals and corresponding to the (002) planes of graphite [32,33]. After loading N-doped graphene quantum dots, the XRD patterns of NG QDs-modified ZnO/NiO hybrids were similar to that of unmodified ZnO/NiO which may indicate low content and high dispersion of NG QDs in NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids. It suggested that deposition process of NG QDs using various methods did not affect the crystalline structure of yolk-shell ZnO/NiO materials.

The composition of elements (at.%) detected by XPS at the surface layer of the as-synthesized ZnO/NiO, NG QDs and all NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids is presented in Table 2 . Fig. 2 shows the high-resolution (HR) spectra of Zn 2p (a) and Ni 2p (b) recorded for ZnO/NiO and for NG-ZnO/NiO microspheres (c,d). The spectra identify well the oxidation states of Zn2+ and Ni2+ in these nanocomposites [26,34,35].

The HR XPS spectra of O 1 s, C 1 s and N 1 s, presented in Fig. 3 , confirm the successful connection of N-doped graphene quantum dots with ZnO/NiO material [27,36]. Both C 1 s and O 1 s deconvoluted spectra disclose the signals originated from ZnO/NiO and N-doped QDs. The N 1 s spectrum of pristine NG QDs contains two peaks located at 399.9 and 401.5 eV, assigned to C-N and C-NH3 (+) species [27,36]. However, an additional nitrogen state is identified in the deconvoluted N 1 s spectra recorded on all NG QDs ZnO/NiO hybrids (Fig. 3). This new nitrogen peak, located at 397.6 eV, can be assigned to C = N and Ni(Zn)-CxHyNz bond formation [36] confirming interaction of NG QDs with ZnO/NiO material. It is important to note that the contribution of this state of nitrogen, related to the total nitrogen surface species in each sample, was found to be 55 %, 45.1 % and 34.8 % for the NG-ZnO/NiO_s, NG-ZnO/NiO_c and NG-ZnO/NiO_a hybrids, respectively (see the N1s spectra in Fig. 3). Meanwhile, the surface composition of elements of all NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids changed compared with the as-synthesized ZnO/NiO and NG QDs (Table 2). The highest content of non-metallic elements (O, C and N) was observed for NG-ZnO/NiO_s sample obtained by solvothermal method and the lowest one for NG-ZnO/NiO_a hybrid prepared by direct adsorption (see (O + N + C)/(Zn + Ni) ratio in Table 2). The amount of Zn surface species becomes higher than Ni as a result of using all three preparation methods. However calcination process change the distribution of Zn and Ni nanoparticles on the surface much more than other preparation routs (compare Zn/Ni ratios in Table 2).

The presence of surface functional groups and interaction between ZnO/NiO hybrids and NG quantum dots were confirmed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The FTIR spectra of obtained materials are presented in Fig. 4 a. All the samples revealed broad absorption bands located at approximately 3000–3500 cm−1 that can be ascribed to O-H stretching vibrations and suggested the appearance of hydroxyl groups and water molecules adsorbed on the surface of materials [37]. ZnO/NiO microsphere and NG QDs modified ZnO/NiO samples showed absorption peak at about 450 cm−1 which can be related to the stretching vibration of Zn-O and Ni-O [38]. The presence of C-N stretching vibration at 1400 cm−1 in the case of NGQDs and NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids indicated the successful doping of nitrogen into graphene QDs and modification of ZnO/NiO with NG QDs [39]. Moreover, the stretching vibration bands of C-O at about 1160 cm−1, 1193 cm−1 and 1300 cm−1 suggested the presence of carboxyl and other oxygen containing groups on the surface of NG QDs [40,41]. In addition, samples containing NGQDs presented characteristic peak at 1580 cm−1 that can be ascribed to the C = O and C = C stretching vibration in the aromatic rings in the structure of NG QDs [42]. It can be also observed that band intensities assigned to QDs (1400 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1) were lowest for NG-ZnO/NiO_a sample and the highest for NG-ZnO/NiO_s hybrids. It indicated that amount of QDs in hybrid materials is dependent on the preparation method. The lowest content of NG QDs for hybrid prepared by direct adsorption and the highest content of NG QDs obtained by solvothermal method is in good agreement with NCHS and XPS analysis.

Thermal properties of as-prepared NG QDs, ZnO/NiO and NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids were investigated and TGA curves are shown in Fig. 4b. The weight loss step below 100 °C can be attributed to the evaporation of water molecules adsorbed on the surface of all the samples. Moreover, as can be seen from this figure, NG QDs decomposed in few steps. The weight loss step occurred at about 100–400 °C can be explained by the removal of carboxyl, hydroxyl and amide groups and combustion of NG QDs [43,44]. The weight loss at higher temperature (above 400 °C) was associated with the relaxation of graphene structure [44,45]. The NG QDs are not completely decomposed at 1000 °C, which may be due to the presence of residual carbon. The carbon nucleus is the main structural unit of NG QDs and exhibits excellent thermostability, resulting in a high residual mass [46]. The NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids exhibited higher thermal stability as compared with pristine quantum dots. The highest weight loss among NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids was observed for NG-ZnO/NiO_s sample and may indicate that solvothermal method allow for deposition of higher amount of quantum dots as compared with calcination and direct adsorption methods, which is in good agreement with NCHS, XPS and FTIR analysis.

The morphology of the obtained materials was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and is presented in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 . As clearly shown (Fig. 5a) ZnO/NiO sample have a well-developed microsphere morphology with diameter of about 1.5–3 μm. Higher magnification image of these particles (Fig. 5b), revealed unique yolk-shell shell structure detected from broken microsphere. It can be clearly seen an internal solid yolk and an outer shell. Fig. 5c-f shows SEM images of N-doped graphene quantum dot-decorated ZnO/NiO hybrids obtained by three various methods. It can be seen that microsphere shape was maintained for NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids obtained by solvothermal (Fig. 5c) and direct adsorption (Fig. 5f) routes. In the case of NG-ZnO/NiO_c sample prepared by calcination method slightly deformed microspheres and partial aggregation of particles were observed. Moreover, for NG-ZnO/NiO_s hybrids (Fig. 5d) microspheres with surface roughness were revealed that can be ascribed to the deposition of NG QDs on the ZnO/NiO sample during solvothermal process.

Fig. 6 shows the TEM images of N-doped graphene quantum dots, ZnO/NiO hybrid and their corresponding composite materials. The average diameter of NGQDs (Fig. 6a) is about 5 nm with a relatively narrow size distribution. The inset of Fig. 6a depicts image of QDs solution with blue emission under UV light. The TEM images shown in Fig. 6c,d,e indicate that after quantum dot modification, the yolk-shell microsphere structure of ZnO/NiO particles was retained. Moreover, the TEM images shown in Fig. 6b-f reveal that the microsphere shell consisted of nanorods. In the case of NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids_c (Fig. 6d) higher tendency to aggregation and reduced shell as compared with NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids obtained by other methods (Fig. 6c,e) were observed indicating that calcination process (400 °C for 2 h) partially destroyedthe original shape of the particles. Higher magnification TEM image (Fig. 6f) of NG-ZnO/NiO_a indicates that the rods of shell contains small nanoparticles. Importantly, TEM images of ZnO/NiO and NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids reveal hollow space between the solid core and nanorod-based shell that can facilitate penetration of electrolyte and accommodate the volume fluctuation upon Li/Na ions insertion and extraction process [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performances of the NG-ZnO/NiO electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: The investigated NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids were tested as anodes for Li-ion and Na-ion batteries. Cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed to exhibit redox activity, galvanostatic charge–discharge test showed the capacity values at various current rates and stability of the materials upon cycling, whereas electrochemical impedance spectroscopy revealed the electronic properties of the electrodes.

CV curves of the investigated hybrids are shown in Fig. 7 a,b and Fig. S1 in in the Supplementary Materials. For all the materials, high irreversible cathodic peak is noticed at about 0.5 V (vs Li/Li+). This peak represents formation of solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI). In the following cycles, broad reversible cathodic peak at about 1 V appears. On the anodic site, two broad peaks at 1.5 and 2.25 V are present. During discharge, conversion of zinc oxide and nickel oxide to metallic zinc and nickel, respectively is expected, as well as alloying of zinc with lithium, as presented by Li et al. [26]:
ZnO + 2Li + 2 e− ↔ Zn + Li2O
Zn + x Li+ + x e− ↔ LixZn
NiO + 2Li+ + 2 e− ↔ Ni + Li2O
Conversion and alloying reactions are reversible as can be seen on the 2nd and 3rd CV cycles.

Comparison of capacity values at different current rates and stability tests over 400 charge–discharge cycles of the investigated ZnO/NiO and NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids are presented in Fig. 7e and f, respectively, respectively. The highest first delithiation capacity value (912 mAh g−1 at 18.6 mA g−1) and the best rate capability (546 mAh g−1 at 1.86A g−1) is presented by sample prepared by the solvothermal method (NG-ZnO/NiO_s). This sample exhibits also the best stability upon prolonged cycling (400 cycles at 372 mA g−1) with average capacity of 525 mAh g−1, which is 16 % higher than obtained for the pristine ZnO/NiO material (Table 3 .). The sample prepared by simple adsorption of N-doped graphene quantum dots (NG-ZnO/NiO_a) shows similar capacity value for the first charge–discharge cycles but the capacity drops significantly upon prolonged cycling, suggesting that the adhesion of the quantum dots to the surface of ZnO/NiO is not strong. The hybrid material prepared by calcination in nitrogen atmosphere (NG-ZnO/NiO_c) presents the lowest capacity values. It may suggest that the thermal treatment deteriorates its structure, influencing the number of active sites for lithium ion storage. Fig. 7g and h present the first and the second charge–discharge curves recorded at small current density (18.6 mA g−1). The Coulombic efficiency of the first cycle is similar for all investigated materials 53–55 % (see Table 3). The highest capacity in the first cycle is shown by NG-ZnO/NiO_a. However, the capacity values for this sample decrease more rapidly in the following cycles than for other materials. The plateau that can be observed on the first lithiation curve correspond well to the irreversible cathodic peak on CV curves, and is attributed to the formation of SEI. In the second cycle the plateau disappear, and the charge–discharge curves of the following cycles overlap each other, like in the case of cyclic voltammetry curves.

ZnO/NiO and NG-ZnO/NiO_s were measured in full cells with lithium iron phosphate as cathode. Due to the high irreversible capacity of the first charge–discharge cycles of the investigated anode materials, the anodes were precycled in a half cells with metallic lithium as counter/reference electrode (20 cycles at 74.4 mA g−1). Despite the precycling of the anodes, the capacity of full cells decreased with each cycle. The achieved reversible discharge energy density values achieved were 107 and 150 Wh kg−1 for ZnO/NiO and NG-ZnO/NiO_s, respectively. This represents a 40 % increase in value for the cell with anode material decorated with N-doped graphene quantum dots.

Impedance spectroscopy measurements were performed for the best performing hybrid sample to investigate the influence of the NG QDs on the electrical properties of the material. Nyquist plots recorded for freshly prepared electrochemical cells (not lithiated electrodes, at potential 3.2 V vs Li/Li+) (Fig. 7i) exhibit semicircle in the high frequency range, followed by capacitive tail at low frequencies. The shapes of Nyquist plots are the same both the samples ZnO/NiO and NG-ZnO/NiO_s. The electric equivalent circuit (EEC) for the pristine samples contains electrolyte resistance R el (∼5 Ω for both samples) connected in series with constant phase element (CPE) in parallel with charge transfer resistance (R C-T) and Warburg diffusion element (Z D). In the case of quantum dot-decorated hybrid the semicircle at high frequency region has smaller dimeter, meaning smaller charge transfer resistance (R C-T). For the NG-ZnO/NiO_s sample R C-T equals 74 Ω, whereas for the pristine ZnO/NiO 93 Ω. This suggest that N-doped graphene quantum dots increase conductivity of the hybrid material. The Nyquist plots of the lithiated electrodes after repetitive cycling (Fig. 7j) show semicircle and diffusive impedance (Warburg element). The EEC of the lithiated electrodes consists of additional element (CPE parallel to resistance), which may be assigned to the formation of SEI. The diameters of the semicircles slightly increase after formation of SEI, however they do not change with cycling. No increase of charge transfer resistance during prolonged charge–discharge processes proves good stability of the electrodes. This suggest that the unique yolk-shell nanostructure provides enough space for volume changes during alloying/de-alloying reactions.

The electrochemical activity towards sodium-ions was also investigated for the materials. Cyclic voltammograms are shown in Fig. 8 a-d and in Fig. S2. in the Supplementary Materials. On the 1st cycle of the CV curves, one can observe three irreversible anodic peaks at 1.1–1, 0.8 and 0.43–0.2 V (vs Na/Na+), suggesting multistep SEI formation and/or unknown irreversible reactions. On the cathodic side, there are two broad overlapping humps at approx. 0.8 and 1.4 V. In the following cycles, one or two broad reversible anodic peaks can be noticed in the potential range between 1 and 0.25 V, and the same cathodic humps as in the first cycle. It can be noticed that the main anodic peaks are slightly shifted towards lower potentials for the quantum dot-decorated hybrids compared to the pristine material. Also, higher current densities are recorded for hybrids compared to the pristine ZnO/NiO. Taking into account lower BET surface area of hybrids compared to the pure ZnO/NiO, the higher currents may be due to enhanced electric conductivity of the hybrids. The current density values recorded in sodium-ion half cells are one order of magnitude lower than in the case of CV curves recorded in lithium-ion cells, which means that all the materials exhibit significantly lower electrochemical activity towards sodium ions compared to Li+.

Rate capability measurements (Fig. 8e) and stability tests (Fig. 8f) show similar electrochemical trends as for lithium ions, but with significantly lower capacity values. The highest capacity values were obtained for NG-ZnO/NiO_s hybrid (235 mAh g−1 at 18.6 mA g−1 and 118 mAh g−1 at 1.86 A g−1) and the lowest for the pristine ZnO/NiO (116 mAh g−1 at 18.6 mA g−1 and 65 mAh g−1 at 1.86 A g−1). After 400 charge–discharge cycles at 372 mA g−1, NG-ZnO/NiO_s and NG-ZnO/NiO_a samples show capacity values of 110 and 120 mAh g−1 at 74.4 mA g−1. Moreover, a comparison between the sodium/lithium storage performance of unmodified and modified ZnO-based anodes reported in the literature with the present work is presented in Table S1 (Supplementary materials). Sinha et al. [48] obtained ZnO@3D-Ni-foam anode that exhibited initial discharge capacity of 175.5 mAh g−1 at 30 mA g−1 and stable ∼ 65.1 mAh g−1 capacity over 400 cycles for Na-ion battery that is lower than those obtained in this study [48]. Much better efficiency was achieved by Gao et al. [49] for nitrogen-doped carbon layer coated ZnO embedded in nitrogen-doped carbon hybrid materials derived from MOFs. The resulting materials exhibited a specific capacity of 320 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles and 110 mAh g−1 after 1000-cycles for Na-ion battery [49]. In summary, there are reports in the existing literature that show both lower and higher capacity and cycle stability values for ZnO-based anodes compared to this study (Table S1). However, this work presents for the first time a novel approach to combine NG quantum dots and MOF-derived ZnO/NiO in the field of Na-ion and Li-ion batteries and demonstrate that:(1) modification of ZnO/NiO hybrid with NG quantum dots enhanced electrochemical performance of anode material, and (2) the coupling method of NG QDs and ZnO/NiO is an important factor that affects the electrochemical performance of the resulting hybrid material.

The measurements for ZnO/NiO and NG-ZnO/NiO_s materials in full cell configuration were conducted using sodium vanadium phosphate as the cathode. The anodes were precycled in the half-cells, similar to the Li-ion full cells. The achieved reversible discharge energy density values were 49 and 61 Wh kg−1 for ZnO/NiO and NG-ZnO/NiO_s, respectively. This represents a 24 % increase in energy density for the cell with the quantum dot decorated anode. Impedance measurements of the materials revealed significantly higher charge-transfer resistances of reactions with sodium ions compared to lithium ions (Fig. S3 in Supplementary Materials), which is probably connected with bigger size of Na+ compared with Li+ (1.02 vs 0.76 Å, respectively) [50].

In summary, NG quantum dot-modified yolk-shell ZnO/NiO microspheres derived from bimetallic MOFs as advanced anodes for lithium/sodium-ion storage were designed and fabricated using three different strategies: direct adsorption, solvothermal route and calcination in N2 atmosphere. The formation of the Zn(Ni)-CxHy chemical bond allowed for successful anchoring of NG QDs to the surface of ZnO/NiO material. Moreover, it was demonstrated that the coupling method of the composite components had significant influence on the electrochemical performance of obtained hybrids. The NG-ZnO/NiO_c composite obtained by calcination presented lower activity than pristine ZnO/NiO and other NG-ZnO/NiO hybrids due to partial deformation of yolk-shell structure by high temperature during preparation process. Meanwhile, the NG-ZnO/NiO_a hybrid prepared by simple adsorption of QDs revealed the capacity drop upon prolonged cycling indicating that adhesion of QDs to the surface of ZnO/NiO was not strong. Among tested samples NG-ZnO/NiO_s material obtained by solvothermal method exhibited the best efficiency: high reversible delithiation capacity of 912mAh/g at 18.6 mA g−1, excellent cycling performance with the average delithiation capacity of 525 mAh/g at 372 mA g−1 over 400 cycles for Li-ion battery and docent electrochemical behavior with capacity values reaching 235 mAh/g at 18.6 mA g−1 for Na-ion battery. The promising electrochemical efficiency of NG-ZnO/NiO_s hybrid can be related to well-developed yolk-shell structure derived from MOFs that accommodated the volume changes during cycling, the presence of N-doped graphene quantum dots that provided more active sites for Li and Na storage, and improved conductivity of electrode, as well as stable coupling of NG QDs with ZnO/NiO hybrid that promoted effective charge-transfer kinetic. The results prove that the structure, composition and the quality of heterojunction between QDs and ZnO/NiO microspheres play important role for the stable and efficient storage of lithium and sodium ions.
